shortTitle: What It’s Like to Be a Hitman Who Missed the Target

content.text: You watch the steam curling in the closed car. You watch it collect on the window, break into beads, slide down the glass to join the dark.
content.imageDescription: Close-up shot from inside a car, focus on steam condensing and dripping down a side window, with darkness outside beyond the wet glass.

content.text: Three hours you’ve been here. You check your watch. You’ve checked it before. You’ll check it again. The clock doesn’t care. The car is cold by now, heat off to avoid the engine noise, breath fogging again with every sigh.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot inside the car, protagonist glancing at their watch. Dashboard clock glows in the dimness, their breath visible in the cold air, interior frosty and silent.

content.text: The gun is in your lap. Black tape on the grip. Jesus piece keychain hanging from the rearview. Not yours, but you don’t touch it. The rules: nothing new, nothing lost.
content.imageDescription: Low shot looking down at the protagonist’s lap: pistol with black-taped grip rests there, a hanging Jesus piece keychain swings gently on the rearview mirror above.

content.text: You open the glove compartment, close it. Inside, the folder. You don’t open it this time; you already know what’s there. Photo, address, routine, notes marked in someone else’s handwriting, clipped and clean. You go over them anyway—in your mind, sequence by sequence. You keep your hands still. You don’t fidget. You wait. That’s the job half the time. People think it’s more. It isn’t.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot from the passenger seat, showing the protagonist opening then closing the glove box, a manila folder just visible inside. Their hands rest motionless on their knees, gaze fixed forward.

content.text: You look at the building across the street. Four stories, windows dark but for that one—third floor, kitchen light on. Target will pass through that window seven times in the hour. You know this because you watched him yesterday. Watched him two days before, from a different car, with a different license plate, always changing just enough.
content.imageDescription: View from inside the car looking out the windshield at a four-story apartment building, all windows dark except one on the third floor glowing warm yellow.

content.text: Your mentor said paranoia was the better twin to pride. He said waiting was the place real mistakes lived. You think about that. You try not to think too much. Discipline is the thing. You breathe.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot of the protagonist sitting in the car, thoughtful, focusing on slow, intentional breaths, expression tense and disciplined.

content.text: Inside, you check the gun. Unholster, check the slide, check the chamber, watch your hands. The pistol is cold and smooth and pitiless in your grip—weight you know precisely. You click the magazine and pull gloves from your coat pocket. Not surgical. Leather, black, tight over your skin. You flex your fingers, counting. You whisper the number to yourself and frown, cut it out, return to silence. The car clock glows. Sleet smacks the windshield.
content.imageDescription: Close-up on their hands: unholstering, checking pistol, sliding in magazine. Leather gloves are put on, fingers flexed in tension, while window shows streaks of sleet outside.

content.text: You remember a night like this, years ago. That time, there was no target. Just you and another man in a stairwell, hands in too-deep pockets, failing to look up. That man isn’t alive now, not because of you.
content.imageDescription: Flashback-style image of a dimly lit stairwell: two figures facing opposite directions, hands deep in coat pockets, tension in the air, but no confrontation.

content.text: You step out. Streetlamp glare chipping at the curb. Your boots crunch the salted concrete, your breath moving in little white clouds. You move with purpose, not fast, never hurried, never slow. You are the person who belongs here, because people who don’t belong look lost, and lost is its own uniform.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of protagonist stepping from car, illuminated by a harsh streetlamp, exhaling visible white breath, boots pressing into gritty, icy sidewalk; figure walks with measured purpose.

content.text: The alley is where you wait. You have the timing right to the second. The boy delivering food brings the key to the back entrance at 1:11 a.m. His shoes squeak. He complains about the cold into the phone. You let him pass.
content.imageDescription: From the shadows of a cold alley, protagonist watches a bundled-up delivery boy approach, take out a key, talk into his phone as he unlocks the back entrance and hustles past.

content.text: You recite the target’s routine. Counts. Seven steps from sofa to kitchen. Open fridge. Remove bottle. Close fridge. Pause. Glass. Pour. Drink. Set glass. Lean against counter. You mouth the words to yourself. Each one in its place. You run through contingencies. You don’t plan for what happens when the target deviates. You never had to.
content.imageDescription: Close shot of protagonist in the alley, lips moving as they silently mouth steps of a memorized routine, eyes focused and analytical.

content.text: You climb the stairs. Each riser creaks in a different way. You learned their rhythm yesterday. You mapped which ones to pressure and which ones to avoid. Third and sixth. You ascend lightly, boots barely brushing, knees soft. There’s a light at the landing. You count your breaths, thumb resting on safety.
content.imageDescription: Interior stairwell: protagonist ascending quietly, skipping the third and sixth steps, bathed in muted light from a small landing bulb, one hand on rail, other on holstered gun.

content.text: Through the narrow gap of the cracked door you see it: the edge of the target’s shadow. Moving, just as predicted. You exhale. You feel the sweat cold under your arms, prickling down your side. That’s ordinary. That’s information. Adrenaline is not a signal; it’s a tool, sharpened to the point of apathy.
content.imageDescription: Point-of-view through a cracked door: edge of a faint shadow moving in kitchen light beyond; protagonist’s hand presses lightly on doorframe, breath condensed in faint mist.

content.text: You hear a voice. Not the target’s. Not supposed to be here. You tense. Footsteps cross the linoleum. Different cadence—longer stride, harder impact. Not in your notes. Not in the folder. Unfamiliar.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist freezes in the narrow hallway outside a half-open flat door, tensing as faint footsteps and a female voice echo beyond, expression shifting to alert concern.

content.text: The voices overlap, laughter cutting through the kitchen’s hush. You recalculate, pulse jamming in your wrist. Abort? Wait. Breathe. This is a variable, not a breach. You have rules for variables. You watch the corridor. The second voice—female, soft, clear, joking about spilled wine, shoes in hand. You imagine the floor slick under her feet.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot inside corridor; protagonist pressed to the wall, peeking at shoes lying in the hall while laughter and a woman's voice drift from inside, their brow furrowed in thought.

content.text: You step back—first step backward in months. Not retreat, adjustment. You check your watch again—reflex. Thirty-seven seconds off schedule. You wait.
content.imageDescription: Low-angle shot showing protagonist quietly stepping backward down the hallway, glancing at their watch, jaw tense, eyes scanning the corridor.

content.text: You try to remember the exit plan, but your mind slips sideways. Gun heavy now, heavier than before. You check the corners, the angles of your approach. Did the doorman see you? Did the camera catch the wrong angle? You trust your checklist. But tonight, trust is a weak shield.
content.imageDescription: Interior shot: protagonist crouching deeper into an alcove, eyes narrowed with worry, glancing towards imagined CCTV camera near ceiling and double-checking the nearby corridor.

content.text: You press deeper into the alcove where you can see without being seen. Key steps echo. Someone’s about to leave. You press your palm to the wall. Cold brick. You push your breath into silence.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of the protagonist pressed into a dark, brick-lined alleyway nook by the apartment entrance, palm flat against cold wall, eyes intent as footsteps approach.

content.text: The door opens. The woman steps out—walking fast, coat half-on, hair tucked behind the ear. She doesn’t turn. She doesn’t see you. She smells of sharp perfume and burnt toast. The details stick.
content.imageDescription: Over-the-shoulder shot from protagonist’s view as a woman in partial dress, coat half-on, quietly slips out a door, hair hastily tucked back, never looking the protagonist’s way.

content.text: You’re alone again. The target is in the window. Light burns yellow on his shoulders. You move forward. Your feet land soundlessly—training matched against nerves. The door is ajar, left by the guest or carelessness. You slide inside. Every sense is swollen now: fridge hum, ticking clock, scrape of glass on counter.
content.imageDescription: Inside the target's dim apartment, protagonist steps through a door left slightly open, padded footsteps near soundless; bright kitchen window silhouette, all senses on heightened alert.

content.text: You see the target. Drinking. Not looking. You raise the gun, align the sights, heart slowed to the beat you drilled for years. Safety off. Finger curled. Exhale. Everything lined up.
content.imageDescription: Tense over-the-shoulder shot: gun raised, sights aligned with target who’s at the kitchen counter, pouring a drink with their back turned, unaware of the danger behind.

content.text: Except the target turns. Not all the way. Not enough to see you full. Just enough to break the pattern. Enough to look up with eyes furrowed, picking something out of the air—a shift, a whisper of cold. Not full alarm, just the precursor.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot: target in profile, pausing mid-action with puzzled, furrowed brows, head slightly turned as if sensing something amiss—protagonist remains in the background shadow.

content.text: He stares out the window. Glass between you, back exposed, but he’s alert now. You sink backwards, body pressed to shadow. This wasn’t in your timing. You recalculate. Windows close in two minutes. You have to move. And suddenly—scrape of shoe, pop of light switch, music from somewhere deeper inside the apartment. He’s not alone after all. Your heart jolts.
content.imageDescription: Wide interior: protagonist hidden just beyond kitchen entrance, target now tense and alert at the window; a new light flares on further inside, hinting at life and music beyond.

content.text: You hear laughter, music, the faux-casual murmur of late company. On nights like this, you call it off. You plan for tomorrow. But the window is closing, this one doesn’t come again. That’s the unspoken risk—one chance, once. Wait longer, and someone else takes the job. Maybe someone you can’t trust not to finish it messy. Maybe the job comes for you next.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist, tense in the shadows of a hallway, listens as laughter and low music mix behind a closed door, expression torn between aborting and seizing the fleeting chance.

content.text: You lean out from shadow, weapon up. Pacing calculated. You’re sweating through your shirt now, vision narrowing to a powder-thin slice. Your hands steady. You pull in breath. You step from cover.
content.imageDescription: Medium, almost first-person shot: protagonist peering from behind cover, gun silhouetted before them, sweat visible on face as they step deliberately into view.

content.text: Except—your shoe catches on something, barely, the softest scrape, but in the silence it echoes, fractures the world. The target turns fast now—sees you, not fully, not clearly, but enough. Liquid sloshes from the glass. Shock flickers across his face. He fumbles with the bottle, drops it.
content.imageDescription: Close shot: protagonist’s shoe snagging on a bottle cap or object, sound echoing. In the background, the startled target whips around, glass and bottle spilling.

content.text: Now, you have no clean angle. He ducks. You fire. Shot muffled by the kitchen curtains, but wild. Glass shatters, but nothing falls except the cheap bottle and the heavy glass. You fire again, but the shot goes wide. He’s already vanished, sprinting for the back room. You taste metal in your mouth. That bitter taste, familiar but hated.
content.imageDescription: Wide shot of chaos in the kitchen: gunshot blows out a glass, bottle and glass clattering. Target vanishes through a back door, while protagonist stands sweating, jaw tight with frustration.

content.text: You’re moving before you think—feet racing, calculating every sound. There’s shouting now, the female voice calling, the door slamming somewhere down the hall. You can’t see the target. Adrenaline rockets, clarity burned out by panic. The corridor is too bright. You lose your footing, catch the wall, count heartbeats.
content.imageDescription: Dynamic interior: protagonist dashes down too-bright corridor, dodges a wall, female voice shouts from an adjacent room, distant door slamming, sense of chaos and stress.

content.text: Abort. You know the word before you let yourself say it. You know what it means. You’ve never needed it before, not in a real job. But tonight, the job is blown. You holster the weapon, wipe the handle with your jacket, push through the exit.
content.imageDescription: Close-up: protagonist slipping gun back in holster, wiping grip clean with jacket, tense face; just outside apartment door, ready to flee.

content.text: Down the stairs—faster, loud this time, you don’t care about the boards creaking. You hear barking, a neighbor’s shout, muffled and meaningless. Feet move without thought. The alley shivers with wind.
content.imageDescription: Action shot: protagonist descends stairs in haste, footsteps loud; a startled neighbor yells from behind a half-open door, distant dog barking as night wind whips through the alley.

content.text: You have rules for this. Drop weapon components at predetermined points. First the magazine in a storm drain, shell casing down a gutter. Gloves bundled, then gone behind a trash bin. You rehearse the sequence in your head. Once, twice, thrice. You check your body for evidence, clothes for stains, mask for warmth. You move like you belong outside. You do not run. You don’t dare.
content.imageDescription: Street-level sequence: protagonist plodding calmly down sidewalk, covertly drops a magazine in a storm drain, shell casing down a gutter, gloves tossed with calculated casualness behind a bin.

content.text: Back in the car, hands trembling now. You grip the wheel till it bites into the skin. You drive exactly the speed limit. You take the city in unfamiliar lines, looping past places you’ve never visited in this persona. You watch your mirrors. You count the headlights behind you. That’s the habit—they always tell you not to look for a tail, just drive. But you can’t help it. Tonight, every shadow could be an answer you don’t want.
content.imageDescription: Nighttime car interior: protagonist white-knuckling steering wheel, glancing tensely into the rearview mirror at headlights behind. City lights blur past outside.

content.text: Home is four neighborhoods and three bridges away. The folder is in the glove box. You don’t look at it, don’t touch it. Routine is fractured now. You close the car door softly. You listen for footsteps. None.
content.imageDescription: Exterior of a nondescript apartment building at night: protagonist parks car, gently closes door, stands still in darkness, listening for footsteps that never appear.

content.text: You stand in your apartment’s entryway, lights off, back to the wall. Ritual—once a comfort, now a mockery. You set the gun parts in a bowl. You sit. You breathe. You count. You fail to calm down.
content.imageDescription: Inside sparse apartment entryway: protagonist drops disassembled gun parts into a bowl, slumps against the wall in the dark, breathing heavily, head bowed.

content.text: You hear your phone buzz. Not the job phone. Your real phone. Name lights up—a brother, half-forgotten and often ignored. He doesn’t call at this hour unless something’s wrong.
content.imageDescription: Tabletop shot: personal phone buzzes on a counter, screen showing a familiar contact name, protagonist hovering a trembling hand over it, anxiety visible.

content.text: You let it ring, counting the seconds. You pick up on the sixth ring, as always. His voice is hushed, taut. He asks if you’re okay. He says someone was looking for you today. He says a man in a dark coat, polite, asked if he knew how to reach you, if you were well, if you kept in touch. Polite questions. The kind that collect answers as warnings.
content.imageDescription: Medium close-up: protagonist holds phone to ear, eyes wide; audio waves from phone suggest worried brother speaking. Tension on face as news of a stranger inquiring is delivered.

content.text: You tell your brother everything’s fine. You hang up. Your hands are damp, cold. Your kitchen clock is ticking out seconds too loudly.
content.imageDescription: Interior shot by kitchen table: protagonist sets phone down, staring at sweating palms, a wall clock in the background dramatically displaying passing seconds.

content.text: You recall your last failure. There was never a last failure. There was never a failure until now. You survived for a reason. You cleaned up your own mistakes as fast as anyone else’s. That's the secret everyone on your side learns: you eliminate your history with efficiency. Only, tonight, the record is incomplete. The window is open. There is a bullet somewhere it should not be. There is a frightened man who’ll tell a story tomorrow, and the wrong ears are always listening when you make noise.
content.imageDescription: Moody shot: protagonist sits at a cluttered desk, replaying the botched hit in their head. A lone bullet sits ominously on the table. Shadows loom heavily in room corners.

content.text: You check your files. You walk through every step again in your mind: car, alley, stairs, back room. You failed to account for the woman, failed to wait for the window to close, failed to mask the sound. You know how mistakes breed: one produces two, then four, then sixteen, and soon the pattern is visible to people you never meant to let see it.
content.imageDescription: Desk shot: protagonist methodically reviews files and clippings, frowning as they retrace each stage; a mental image shows branching 'mistakes' multiplying, represented by spreading shadows.

content.text: You unpack the kill-bag, sort everything by habit, hands moving without looking. You put the gun together, no bullets. You wonder if there’s time to finish the job later. Wonder if, by trying, you’ll only make things worse. The sense of opportunity closing is like a vice: slow, unforgiving, inevitable.
content.imageDescription: Close-up: protagonist methodically sorts through a black duffel, lays out gear, reassembles an empty pistol. Their eyes linger on the weapon, expression conflicted and resigned.

content.text: You get a call. Tonight, it is not the dispatcher. It is your handler. Never calls after midnight. He doesn't greet you. He says the job wasn’t clean. He says the client is unhappy. He says the client is disappointed. Emphasis on disappointed, not angry. You understand the code.
content.imageDescription: Close-up of phone screen, late at night, showing incoming call from withheld number. Protagonist answers, stone-faced, as vague but ominous words are received through the earpiece.

content.text: You’re told to sit tight. Not to leave town. That there will be a follow-up meeting. Those words mean something, in this world. They mean you are being measured for damage. They mean your usefulness is no longer perfect. The call disconnects. You stare at the dark phone and wonder who is coming next.
content.imageDescription: Medium shot: protagonist hunches over in chair, phone clasped tight, face illuminated by screen’s glow. The phone goes dark, leaving them alone in the gloom, eyes filled with dread.

content.text: You replay the evening. Seven times. Eight. You find each moment where a different breath, a different glance, could have changed the outcome. You try not to let your mind drift into blame or regret, but it does anyway. That’s the risk—the invisible cost.
content.imageDescription: Back view: protagonist at small table, head bowed in hands, multiple ghostly overlays show them replaying choices and moments in their mind, burdened by regret.

content.text: You sit at your table, head in your hands. You have the address of the target memorized, his schedule etched behind your eyes, but that means nothing now. The routine failed you. You failed it. You took a contract that was supposed to go quietly and left the world a little louder instead. Now you are waiting. You hate waiting. The hours are measured in heartbeats and imagined footsteps behind you. In phone calls that might never come, or worse—calls that come when you’re not ready.
content.imageDescription: Dim-lit kitchen: protagonist sits alone, head buried in hands, darkness and isolation heavy. A slip of paper with the target's contact info sits untouched beside them; an imagined flicker of movement is at the door.

content.text: You think about running. New city. New name. Leaving the bag, the gun, everything behind. You know people who tried. You know where they ended up. You know there is no good place to hide from your own reputation. Besides, you are tired. Tired in the way only a mistake makes people tired—bone deep and cold.
content.imageDescription: Contemplative shot: protagonist stares out apartment window, overnight city stretching far below, duffel bag and weapon on table; facial expression drained and deeply weary.

content.text: You get up. Pour yourself a drink. You stare at the wall. The smell of powder and burnt metal still in your nostrils. You listen for the sound of approaching cars, the chime of the elevator, the clatter of footsteps outside your door. None of those sounds come, and that is somehow worse.
content.imageDescription: Protagonist stands in shadowy kitchen, pours liquor into a glass, gazes at a blank wall. Ears strained for any hint of an approach—hallway outside remains silent.

content.text: You return to your routine. You clean the dishes, wipe the counters, pretend you are just someone who cares about neatness. You aren’t, not anymore. That fiction is gone. You finish the drink, set the glass down without a sound.
content.imageDescription: Low shot of kitchen counter: protagonist quietly cleans the sink and counters, moves like on autopilot, finishes drink, and places glass delicately on countertop, all in silence.

content.text: You look at your face in the kitchen window—fragmented by city lights, pale and drawn. You don’t recognize it. You count the scars. You try to smile for practice. You fail.
content.imageDescription: Close-up reflection: protagonist’s drawn, pale face and visible scars gaze out at the city through window glass, a weak smile flickering and then falling away.

content.text: You write a name on a slip of paper. Not the target’s name. The handler’s. Just in case. You consider what a confession would buy you, if anything. You crumple the paper and throw it out. You wait. Time passes. Every tick is an accusation, every hour a sentence. You sit. You pray—the kind of prayer that is less to a god and more to the shadow that you hope is friendly on the far side of the glass.
content.imageDescription: Interior: protagonist scribbles a name on scrap paper in shaky hand, then crumples and throws it toward a wastebin. They slump at the table, hands folded, lost in a wordless, desperate prayer.

content.text: Tomorrow you may get the call. Tomorrow you may not. But tonight, you’re still here, surrounded by fragments of a life you built out of silence and precision, brought low by a single, echoing mistake. The hit never landed. The window never closed. So you wait for the door to open—by you, or by them, you don’t know which will come first.
content.imageDescription: Final scene: medium shot of protagonist sitting in a dark apartment, amidst scattered tools of the trade, staring at the closed door, uncertain whether to rise or brace for intrusion.
